feed.title: Kiyomizu’s Homepage
feed.subtitle: Matthew 7:13 “Enter in by the narrow gate; for the gate is wide and the way is broad that leads to destruction, and there are many who enter in by it.

entry.title: 本月评论
entry.content:
11月5日

下午看了《西线无战事》（2022版），一部很好的战争片。士兵为了无意义的东西死去的情节让我想起了《一个德国人的故事》。

据说1930年版更好，但世界大战还是发生了，可见人类并没有吸取教训。

11月6日

《旧制度与大革命》是一本历史研究著作，不可以当成故事读。

曹丕的断句

回头四向望，眼中无故人。

颇为感人，大概是指建安二十二年（公元217年），“年疾疫，亲故多离其灾，徐陈应刘，一时俱逝，痛可言邪！”。

11月7日

《中国古代文学》课上，拟谢灵运“池塘生春草，园柳变鸣禽”、“林壑敛瞑色，云霞收夕霏”作诗：

朝阳发蒸汽，水雾散晨光。
池塘生春草，台阶长小猫。

上课时读曹丕《典论·论文》和《与吴质书》，摘录片段.

《典论·论文》

盖文章经国之大业，不朽之盛事。年寿有时而尽，荣乐止乎其身。二者必至之常期，未若文章之无穷。

《与吴质书》

昔伯牙绝弦于锺期，仲尼覆醢于子路，愍知音之难遇，伤门人之莫逮也。诸子但为未及古人，自一时之隽也，今之存者已不逮矣。后生可畏，来者难诬，然吾与足下不及见也。

entry.title: Notes of Reading, II, La Production Des Faits Scienfiques

link.title: Notes of Reading, II, La Production Des Faits Scienfiques

entry.content:
Three days after posting my last comment and quote, I’m finishing the book Life in the Lab. Although the book was first published in 1986, more than three decades later, its views are still not out of date, even ahead of their time. Understanding these perspectives can inspire new thinking about a research career. Therefore, although I have already recommended it last time, I still want to recommend it to all freshmen and sophomores of the School of Biological Sciences, especially those who have not yet joined the laboratory.

Last time, I briefly introduced the content of the first three sections of this book and quoted some fragments. Here I will introduce the content of the last three sections of this book and quote some new content.

As the successor of Chapter 3, Chapter 4 “Microsociology of Facts” has the characteristics of a theoretical work in social science. If the third chapter is “Anatomy of a Sparrow”, then the fourth chapter should be called “Ornithology”. Briefly, there are two points of view:

  1. Facts exist, just as TRF does exist;
  2. Facts are constructed, as in the TRF construction process described in Chapter 3.

The two views are not contradictory. Specifically, in 1968, thyrotropin-releasing factor (TRF) was just a statement, which could be a fact or an illusion; by 1970, TRF had become a fact, and people were saying “a team found that TRF”. The research debate of 1968-1970 led to the confirmation of the existence of TRF, and this process was construction.

The fact that “the structure of TRF is Pro-Glu-His-Pro-NH2” is thought to be the reason for the end of the debate, as it stands now. But these people are hindsight. Because once you make a hypothesis: “If no one has found the correct structure of TRF so far, and the academic world is arguing continuously”, then “The structure of TRF is Pro-Glu-His-Pro-NH2” will be the end of this debate in the future time results.

That is, it is the debate and research that constructs the facts , not the facts that define the debate. However, I only agree with this in sociological discussions, because it’s a lot like sophistry, and you can use magic against magic.

But compared to this, Chapter 5, “The Credibility of the Scientist”, is much more real. This chapter discusses: What exactly is a researcher pursuing in his/her research career? I think a lot of students don’t think clearly. If you take this question into a career interview, you may get very rich answers, but you need to judge the value of these answers for yourself. I would like to quote a passage from the author to illustrate the problem:

When Nathan got his technicians ready to assemble the next identification instrument, he said: ‘If we don’t do two tests, people will say that the data in our article is derived from other methods’… Later when someone asked Nathan why he used this When the instrument was used, he replied: ‘No amount of caution is too much for science! ‘

This question should be asked in a different way: what drives researchers to do research/change topics/change jobs?

Is it because of material rewards? Is it because of awards and honors? Is it to contribute? The author partially agrees with every answer here. But he went a step further and put forward a concept, credit (literally translated as credibility, confusing, but this confusion is helpful to avoid readers taking the literal meaning, and it is also a good translation).

Take a look at the following cycle. This is how the capital needed by a scientific researcher to achieve achievements in the field is converted from one form to another.

Good researchers can keep this cycle running, and credit is the energy for this cycle to run. E.g:

People with high credit, with more funding, better equipment, more data, more arguments, better papers, more explanations, more Rewards, you can manage more funds…and so on. Some people go uphill, some go downhill. Every step in this step affects credit, such as winning a Nobel Prize, getting a large amount of money (for academic or other reasons), getting critical equipment disabled, being found to be falsified, and publishing important papers. Credit skillfully integrates various elements. From my superficial understanding, it includes at least: academic reputation, academic reputation, personal connections, educational background, ability recognition, honor, research prospects, and the ability to apply for funding. If you insist on changing the credit to a more appropriate translation, I think “the ability to acquire academic capital” is very suitable. In short, all efforts, all struggles, are to improve this ability.

Those who can’t do that are out, because it’s the iron law of survival of the fittest.

The following is quoted from Chinese translation1:

As we have seen, the production of reliable data is a means of enlivening the cycle of credibility and the “scientific trade” or what Foucault called the “real political economy” in 1978. Thereafter, researchers can do their best to use their credibility in their own name. They can say with certainty that they “had an idea” (see p. 147 of this book). pages and below), which concerns “their” laboratories, which they succeeded in attracting funds to equip, thus securing the basis of their activities. From this point of view, they are similar to businessmen at the same time as government employees. However, it is important that their capital cannot be sold and inherited, and only in rare cases can be exchanged for money capital. Like artisans, they work to produce their own data, and they manage their own almost exclusive accounts. However, if they are not careful, they will be reduced to clerks or senior technicians. They can also be independent, and if they are lucky, they can also be employers. But at the same time, they are still employees because they are paid to manage private funds or money lent to them by taxpayers. The researchers we’ve observed are, financially speaking, underwhelming: they have to constantly manage the credibility capital at their disposal, while also having to constantly adjust the use of money and trust lent to them. A tense atmosphere always hangs over the operating laboratory, which constantly motivates people to come up with a new thesis, to prove it, to promote its utility, to install new instruments, to exploit the merits and to reinvest it. In times of crisis, the tension that cheers up a combat unit staff or decision-making body is nothing compared to the atmosphere that surrounds the laboratory every day! This tension forced people to try their best to persuade the secretary to type the manuscript on time, ask the technician to order the animals and equipment for the experiment as quickly as possible, and urge people to do the experiment carefully. Of course, this tension is often encountered in other production units. However, in the laboratory, the nature of this atmosphere is that it forces the researcher to be reliable. On the one hand, the researcher has to face the constraints of the investor, and he has to constantly reinvest his capital if he doesn’t want to lose his capital. On the other hand, they also have to take on the obligations of their employees, because people always demand that the funds lent to them be paid off. Under these two pressures, our researchers stayed in the lab. If a researcher decides to stop conducting new experiments, or make new claims, he is quickly eliminated. He’ll never get funding again, and he’ll be kicked out unless he has tenure, or he’s already built a nest for himself. It is possible, but redundant, to explain researchers’ behavior in terms of “norm,” the scientific method, or the pursuit of rewards. The force that binds the researcher, who is both an independent capitalist and an employee, is that the same firm position can only be maintained by producing hard facts.

Unlike fellows, technicians have no initial credibility capital (PhD title) at their disposal. Even if technicians were not as interested in building and reinvesting scientific credibility as they were in salary, they did show concern in how merit was distributed and rewarded. From an economic point of view, technicians are more like workers than investors. Their salaries repaid their labor, but did not constitute investable capital. Nonetheless, there is no denying that they will use various strategies to change their fate, such as changing laboratories. However, this transfer has never been the same as an investor with a Ph.D. This situation explains why a large number of technicians leave the laboratory to take remedial courses for their Ph.D. During our stay in the lab, five technicians were transferred for this purpose. Technicians hope that by earning this qualification, their work will bring them a lucrative salary as well as a growing credibility that may be reinvested.

Madeleine sees a “senior technician” as a qualified researcher who undertakes simple, routine tasks. Since many full-time fellows with PhDs spend a lot of time working as technicians, the advantages of PhDs are minimal, she argues. For her, the difference between a technician and a senior technician is not enough to make up for years of hard work. So what is special about a PhD holder?



Kiyomizu

November 7th, 2022


Bibliography:

  1. Latour B., Woolgar S., ZHANG Bolin, and DIAO Xiaoyin. La Production Des Faits Scienfiques. Beijing: Eastern Press, 2004. ↩
entry.summary: Three days after posting my last comment and quote, I’m finishing the book Life in the Lab. Although the book was first published in 1986, more than three decades later, its views are still not out of date, even ahead of their time. Understanding these perspectives can inspire new thinking about a research career. Therefore, although I have already recommended it last time, I still want to recommend it to all freshmen and sophomores of the School of Biological Sciences, especially those who have not yet joined the laboratory.

entry.title: Notes of Reading, I, La Production Des Faits Scienfiques

link.title: Notes of Reading, I, La Production Des Faits Scienfiques

entry.content:
Today I read “Lab Life: The Construction of Scientific Facts” by Bruno Latour and Steve Woolgar. This is a very good book. It is recommended for every student from the School of Biological Sciences or Yuanpei to read it, especially those who have not joined the laboratory in their freshman year and sophomore year. The gain from reading it is greater than The Double Helix recommended by the reading students’ overview course.

The authors of the book provide an in-depth study of Roger Guillemin’s lab at the Salk Institute, providing an anthropological perspective on scientific research, publications, competition, laboratory structures, and scientists’ careers. The authors quite aptly summarize these aspects as “laboratory life”. I don’t know the field of the two authors, but Google Scholar shows that the book has more than 20,000 citations, which at least partially explains the book’s impact.

Roger Guillemin was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1977. He and Andrew V. Schally were awarded for their discovery that the brain can produce peptide hormones. Their work focused on the identification of the structures of thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH) and gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH). This work should be at an intermediate level compared to other work that has won the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine at the same time. In addition, the Salk Institute, the site of the survey, remains a prestigious institution in the life sciences to this day. So this is a pretty good sparrow.

The book’s most eye-catching chapter is Chapter 3, “Making Facts: The Case for Thyroid-Stimulating Factor-Releasing Factor [TRF(H)],” It may offend scientists or future researchers: We learn to distinguish opinion from fact at an early age, how can you say that scientific facts are made? !

But in this example, you can clearly see this “manufacturing” process, which is real, cold, and impure. Not only “eight years of hard work”, “extracting a few micrograms of hormones from tons of hypothalamus”, “interdisciplinary”, but also “creating strict rules to exclude competitors, Scheduling academic meetings to safeguard the interests of the laboratory and “faithful researchers who learn from the mistakes of others”. This chapter successfully restores the history of the field in the 1960s-1970s, and several of its conclusions have been written into current physiology textbooks. I think if there were a “physiology discussion class” this would be an excellent reference.

This book is available in English, French and Chinese translation. The Chinese translation was published by Oriental Publishing House in 20041. The translation quality is poor, but it is still difficult to conceal the insight in the content and the professionalism of the author. But unfortunately, one of the authors, Bruno Latour, passed away half a month ago, and the philosopher died. Below are some excerpts posted, all of which made me feel at ease during the reading process.

The following quote is from the Chinese translation1:

Page 33 line 11 - Page 35 2nd last line:

多次目击瓷砖实验台的工作情况，我们的观察者相信，在那里工作的人甚至以强迫自己的、工作狂的方式进行写作。所有的瓷砖工作台都有一个皮封面登记簿，小组成员在那上面用代码细致地通报他们刚做的事。这种奇怪的举动使我们的观察者回忆起某些擅长细腻描写情节的小说家，由千担心遗忘，他们不得不把他们看到的一切都记下来。其实在他看来，当技术人员不再调整那些极复杂的仪器时，他们就花时间在纸上把数字排成长长的数列；如果不是在纸上记载，就是在一些试管壁上或老鼠的皮肤上做记号，他们花费大量时间来登记这些数字。有时，他们也利用有色纸带给烧杯或一排排表面闪光的外科手术台贴上标识。这种奇特的登记怪癖表现为产生大量的卡片、资料和词典，因此，除了语言词典和著名的缩氨酸词典，人们还找到大家所说的《材料词典》。例如照片2 就是储存载满标本试管的制冷机，每个试管都有10 位数的代码号标签。大量的化学用品按字母表的顺序摆在多层架上，这给技术人员的操作提供了方便。更惊人的例子是关于材料的《词典》，成套的报刊文章（照片14 ，下图）和数千张数据卡片，其中每张卡片都再次编上索引代码号。此外还应该加上各种资料（发票、支票、计划、财产清单、信函卷宗等等），它们都分门别类地摆放，简直就像在所有企业里一样。

从瓷砖实验台到办公室，观察者重又陷入这样的世界：在那里，写出的东西更加含蓄。办公桌上堆满了词条的复印件。某些词还被划上着重线，白边上写满了惊叹号。文章的手稿靠近手边一张张速描的图解一一同事的信件、来自相邻房间的印刷名册，剪下来的论文被贴到其他论文上，准备中的论文摘录一个人一个人地传阅，比较超前的观点在办公室之间交流；从这个或那个刊物上剪下来的论文不断地被修改、修饰、重新更正。A 区的成员不在纸上写写画画时，他们就在黑板上写东西，口述信件或准备他们下一次报告的幻灯片。

这样，我们人类学家观察者就遇到了一个奇怪的部落，这个部落正度过自己编码、做标记、读与写的最光辉的时代。从表面看，这些活动与做标记、书写、编码和修订并无关系，那么，这些活动有什么意义呢？例如，我们在照片4 上看到两位照管老鼠的年轻妇女。即使我们注意到有一页议定书在右边，在试管架上有一些编好号码的试管，在背墙上有一只用来测定试验时间长短的钟，但是她们并没有在读或写。左边的妇女用一个注射器注射一种液体，她用另一只注射器吸入另一种液体，并把它递给她的同事，这位同事把这注射器中的液体注入一个试管中。这仅仅是书写阶段的开始：她们认真记下花费的时间和试管的号码。在这期间，一些动物被宰杀，而各种物品——乙酰、棉花、吸管和注射器，都用上了。这些动物出于什么理由被宰杀呢？使用这些东西与书写活动之间有何关系？对试管架上的东西进行仔细的检查并没使我们的观察者把这一切弄得更清楚。在几天之中，试管都排成行，在里面加注了其他的液体，这些混合液经搅动后被放入制冷机里。操作和重新进行试管分类的常规按阶段中止。从老鼠身上摘取的标本被送入一个仪器里，它们在那里要经受一项彻底的转变：不改变标本也不给它们贴标签，机器吐出了打满字的纸页（照片6) 。一个人扯掉打印机出来的纸页，经过认真检查后他决定，试管重新处理。换句话说，这些试管虽然经过一周的时间认真加工（编号码一一在工时和材料上耗费数千法郎的活动），从此就丧失了一切价值。人们只对写满数字的这页纸感兴趣。在前面的研究中我们这位幸运地体验过实验的观察者，已经遇到了其他人既不合乎逻辑、又古怪的行为。相对说来，我们的观察者受震动还算少，他在考虑，他的下一次惊奇会是什么。

令人惊奇的事不久就出现了。这张写满数字的纸即漫长的检验的最终产物被当做资料输入电脑（照片11) ；打印机打出一个新清单，这就是新的考虑对象：这就是总过程的重要成果。至千最初那份列表，它是从数千个其他充斥各图书馆的类似列表归纳出而被归档的。因此，一系列变换并没有结束。照片12 说明一位技术人员正在研究电脑下载的不同列表。一会儿照相就完成了，有人招呼他进入办公室，在那里人们让他看他努力的成果：在方格计算纸上画出的一条简洁明了的弧线。人们的注意力又一次被转移了，列表被归入档案，在办公室里，有人热情地介绍曲线的最高点和坡度：”这多么惊奇！”“这就是已分化的峰值！”“它全速下降！”“这两点何其相似！”几天以后，观察者眼前就出现一个准备发表的重画的、同样的弧线的新翻版。一旦论文被接受，它将被未来的读者看到，人们随时都能在其他办公室里找到它，在那里，它有助于更新并列的过程和文献的结构。

Page 58 line 11-20:

被提纯的物质和罕见的抗血清剂也被看做重要的王牌。当发生小组某个成员说要离开小组这样的事时，他流露出对他所负责的抗血清剂、小块制剂和样品的担忧。正是这些添加到他写的论文中的东西成了他的王牌，这些王牌使他能够在外面被肯定，并继续他的写作活动。他有各种机会在别处寻找类似的记录器，而不是寻找可以进行这样或那样的放射免疫测试的专用的抗血清剂。除了样品外，实验室还培养本领；获得了本领的小组成员不时地离开实验室以便在别处施展自己的才华。再说一遍，本领只是达到最高目标——创作论文——的手段。

Page 182 line 19 - Page 184 line 8:

人们由此可以看到，科研职业的启动，意味着一系列的决定，通过这些决定，每个人都在逐渐地构筑起他自己的品行、能力证明之库。这些证明就是将来别人评估迪特里希并可能向他投资的参考。

“我参加过在埃拉特举行的会议……我知道神经生理学的好处……我感到这是一个好的、负担不过重的研究领域，肯定会越来越重要……不像癌症的研究：一旦人们找到了治愈的方法，科研也就结束了。［记事簿XI,85]”

迪特里希解释说他决定从事神经生理学的研究是由于他从中看到了”好处＂。而我们从这些话中也察觉到，一个年轻的科研人员判断某一领域为他提供的前途和摆在他面前的机遇时，所用的几乎是经济学的计算方法。他也正是用这种方法来判断着从己作出的投入中，作为回报他所能获得某些东西的机遇。对千迪特里希来说，下一步就是挑选一个在这个研究领域工作的人的问题了。

“在这次会议上我听到人们谈论X 。我去拜访他，但是，最终他拒绝接受我……他不要医生……他无意于培养年轻人的科研小组……认为这是浪费时间。［记事簿XI,65]”

在会议上听到的议论，使迪特里希确信X 是本专业最优秀的人才。也就是说，在有同等学历的清况下， X 小组的人显得比任何其他小组得到的都多。招聘的过程就是一场谈判，双方都试图估价对方可能为自己提供什么样的资本。

“X 建议我去拜访Y 。在其研究所， Y 对我说：＂研究此课题在一年内你可以步入坦途，我将设法使你在……谋到一个长期的职位＂。研究课题是确定一种酶在大脑中的部位……，在时间方面，他犯了一个大错误，因为至今这还是一个尚待解决的难题。但是，我需要一个职位，我听从了他的劝告……我在……得到了一个职位，我发表了许多文章。［记事簿Xl,85]”

这是一个职业生涯软启动的例子。记录设备为迪特里希提供了大量的资料，足够他写论文和发表文章。总之， Y 的投入结出了果实。然而，就奖励而言，迪特里希得到的很少。迪特里希的工作没有得到广泛的承认，人们并不认为他的工作有什么突出之处。但是， Y 的支持足以使他可以找到一个职位。他成了一个被信任的研究人员，一个有能力在其领域内从事严谨工作的人。



Kiyomizu

October 27th, 2022


Bibliography:

  1. Latour B., Woolgar S., ZHANG Bolin, and DIAO Xiaoyin. La Production Des Faits Scienfiques. Beijing: Eastern Press, 2004. ↩ ↩2
entry.summary: Today I read “Lab Life: The Construction of Scientific Facts” by Bruno Latour and Steve Woolgar. This is a very good book. It is recommended for every student from the School of Biological Sciences or Yuanpei to read it, especially those who have not joined the laboratory in their freshman year and sophomore year. The gain from reading it is greater than The Double Helix recommended by the reading students’ overview course.

entry.title: Memories of life, I
entry.content:
Reading and writing book notes were an integral part of my life for several years before and after high school. At that time, I liked to read “Reference News”, “Life Weekly” and “Dushu” magazines. I always went to the bookstore near the school, to the school library for the books that hadn’t been loaned out for decades. However, recently, WeChat official account articles, bilibili videos and Zhihu answers have become my main sources of information. In addition to the materials that I have to write, I seldom pick up a pen.

These words sound like nostalgic clichés: the past was slower, more enjoyable, and purer than the present. Maybe, but I don’t think so. Although some aspects of the world are on the decline, my life is generally better than it was in the past. If you give me a chance to return to the age of sixteen or seventeen, I don’t want to, because I was too lonely at that time.

In order to relieve loneliness, I read boring books, write reading notes with titles, talk about things beyond my experience from my limited life experience, and try to pretend to be stubborn old man in a young age. In a word: Beautiful but muddled So the “purity” at that time was caused by the current situation, even accidental, not an ideal state. But I still want to thank the person who has guided me to read books that have shaped my way of looking at things to a large extent, making me different and distinct from my peers in both good and bad ways.

My high school Chinese teacher is a wise educator. “Education is not about indoctrination, but lighting the torch”; “Education is not about dragging, but one cloud pushes another”, which is her portrayal. She recommended us many good books, attached gentle encouraging comments on each of our reading notes. Even now it seems that my essays are lacking in style, and dogmatic in thinking. †

Since college, I have had the idea many times: “Besides study and work, I should re-read some books I am interested in and write some reading notes”, but I have never insisted on it for more than three days. The reason is no more than: too busy, troublesome, no one is watching. The first is a hypocritical excuse, because there is obviously a lot of time to spend on electronic information flow; the second may have some truth, since college has been “running to school”, and there is often only one computer in the bag. But there is no pen and paper; the third point is real, I used to have at least one serious reader, but now I often don’t dare to show the people around me because the things I write are too hypocritical, and I don’t think I should. waste their time.

I learned a lot of knowledge and principles in college, the most important of which is to “treat real life pragmatically”. Reading has been used by me as a means of escaping the truth, but after so many days, reading should take a different attitude now. In order to maintain balance, it is good to leave some leisure gaps in addition to acquiring knowledge, training ability, and a rich resume. Instead of letting the clutter of videos and news smear these blanks, it’s better to read a little interesting book and write your own notes.

About a year ago, I first learned about two ancient blogging sites, Richard Stallman’s Personal Site and Lu Changhai’s Personal Page. They are all over two decades old and carry a large amount of text, including essays, photos, memories, opinions, and excerpts from readings. I think this is a good form, so I set up this static website and plan to update it for a long time. Maybe in the vast Internet, there will be people who come to this site by accident.

Different from the above two, the content of this site is limited to the notes of reading, life memories and a little knowledge. I will not share the current life or specific identification information here. But if you’d like to discuss or share interesting books, my email is kiyomizu.e@foxmail.com, letters are welcome.



Kiyomizu

October 7th, 2022
entry.summary: Reading and writing book notes were an integral part of my life for several years before and after high school. At that time, I liked to read “Reference News”, “Life Weekly” and “Dushu” magazines. I always went to the bookstore near the school, to the school library for the books that hadn’t been loaned out for decades. However, recently, WeChat official account articles, bilibili videos and Zhihu answers have become my main sources of information. In addition to the materials that I have to write, I seldom pick up a pen.
